1:
On James 5:1 — “Go to now, ye rich men, weep and howl for your miseries that shall come upon you” — the witnesses mark the denunciation of the ungodly rich.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the address. Robertson: “come now, ye rich” (age nyn), the rich as a class. Ellicott: “howling for your miseries” (cf. Isaiah 13:6), a picture word. The Reformed Calvin: not an exhortation to repent but a denunciation of God’s judgment. Wesley: spoken for the sake of the oppressed poor, not the rich themselves.

The verse opens a woe on the wicked rich. They are bidden to weep and howl for coming miseries. Judgment, not repentance, is announced.

So the witnesses see James open his closing section with a thunderous denunciation of the oppressive rich. The witnesses are careful to note that not all rich men are meant: some are godly and use their wealth well; the target is the wicked, oppressing rich who hoard and defraud. They mark the arresting summons: “go to now, ye rich men” — the same attention-call as before, now turned on this class. They observe the command of dread: “weep and howl” — not the quiet weeping of repentance but the loud howling of anguish, the wailing of those facing doom; the word, as Ellicott notes, is a picture of terror drawn from the prophets. They note the cause: “for your miseries that shall come upon you” — the judgments of God soon to fall, suddenly and unexpectedly. They observe Calvin’s point that this is not, like the call to the worldly in chapter 4, an exhortation to repent, but a simple denunciation of judgment, meant to terrify; and Wesley’s, that it is spoken chiefly for the comfort of the oppressed poor, who were groaning under these men’s cruelty. They note that the rich are addressed as a warning to them and an assurance to their victims. The verse thus sounds the woe: the ungodly rich are summoned to wail over the miseries about to overtake them — the grounds of which the next verses detail.

So the witnesses see James open his closing section with a denunciation of the oppressive rich. Not all rich men are meant: some are godly; the target is the wicked who hoard and defraud. The arresting summons “go to now, ye rich men” is turned on this class. The command of dread: “weep and howl” — not the quiet weeping of repentance but the loud howling of anguish, a prophetic picture of terror. The cause: “your miseries that shall come upon you” — God’s judgments soon to fall. Calvin notes this is not, like the call to the worldly in chapter 4, an exhortation to repent, but a denunciation meant to terrify; and Wesley, that it is spoken chiefly for the comfort of the oppressed poor groaning under these men’s cruelty.
2:
On James 5:2 — “Your riches are corrupted, and your garments are motheaten” — the witnesses mark the rotting of hoarded wealth.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the imagery. Robertson: “are corrupted” (sesepen, perfect of sepo, to rot), of perishable wealth. Adam Clarke: “riches” here the produce of fields and flocks, grain, wine, oil — which can putrefy. Ellicott: wealth mouldering in corruption, garments moth-eaten. The Reformed Calvin: he ridicules their foolish confidence in fading riches.

The verse pictures their wealth decaying. Their hoarded riches rot and their garments are moth-eaten. Treasure stored up uselessly is treasure destroyed.

So the witnesses see James portray the very wealth the rich trusted as already rotting. The witnesses note the first form: “your riches are corrupted” — the perfect tense picturing them as already putrefied; as Clarke explains, the riches of the ancients lay largely in stores of grain, wine, and oil, which spoil when hoarded uselessly. They mark the second: “your garments are motheaten” — the costly wardrobes laid up in vain superfluity, eaten by moths while the poor went unclothed. They observe the force of the picture: the wealth heaped up and withheld does not enrich but decays; its very accumulation, beyond use, becomes its ruin. They note Calvin’s point that James ridicules their foolish confidence, for the riches in which they placed their happiness are fading and could be reduced to nothing by one breath of God. They observe that the rotting goods are a silent indictment: what should have fed the hungry and clothed the naked was selfishly hoarded until it spoiled. They note that the decay of the treasure foreshadows the doom of those who trusted it. The verse thus exposes the folly of hoarding: the riches and garments selfishly stored are corrupting and moth-eaten — perishing testimony, as the next verse declares, against their owners.

So the witnesses see James portray the rich man’s wealth as already rotting. The first form: “your riches are corrupted” — the perfect tense picturing them as putrefied; as Clarke explains, the ancients’ riches lay largely in stores of grain, wine, and oil, which spoil when hoarded uselessly. The second: “your garments are motheaten” — costly wardrobes laid up in vain superfluity, eaten by moths while the poor went unclothed. The wealth heaped up and withheld does not enrich but decays; its very accumulation, beyond use, becomes its ruin. Calvin notes James ridicules their foolish confidence, for the riches in which they placed their happiness are fading and could be reduced to nothing by one breath of God. The rotting goods are a silent indictment.
3:
On James 5:3 — “Your gold and silver is cankered; and the rust of them shall be a witness against you… Ye have heaped treasure together for the last days” — the witnesses mark the rust that testifies against the rich.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figure. Robertson: “are rusted” (katiotai, rusted through). Ellicott: the precious metals do not corrode, but the base alloy does — the rust a witness. Adam Clarke: the rust a witness of covetousness, keeping all to themselves. Wesley: “ye have heaped treasure… will eat your flesh as fire.”

The verse turns their treasure into a witness. Their cankered gold’s rust testifies against them, heaped up for the last days. Hoarded riches accuse their owners.

So the witnesses see James make the rich man’s very treasure rise up to accuse him. The witnesses note the image: “your gold and silver is cankered” — rusted through from long disuse; though, as Ellicott observes, pure gold does not truly corrode, the figure stands for wealth so hoarded that it might as well have rusted, the base alloy tarnishing. They mark the testimony: “the rust of them shall be a witness against you” — the very corrosion of the unused treasure bears witness in God’s court to the owner’s covetousness, his selfish hoarding while others starved. They observe the dreadful added clause: it “shall eat your flesh as it were fire” — the rust that consumed the metal becomes a figure of the judgment that will consume the hoarder, the corrosion turning to torment. They note the final indictment: “ye have heaped treasure together for the last days” — in the very last days, when they should have been laying up treasure in heaven, they amassed perishable wealth on earth, storing up not riches but wrath. They observe Clarke’s and Wesley’s sense that the buried talents, hoarded instead of used for good, testify to their guilt. The verse thus completes the charge of hoarding: the cankered gold and its rust stand as witness and torment against the rich who heaped treasure for the last days — and a further crime, defrauding the laborer, the next verse names.
4:
On James 5:4 — “Behold, the hire of the labourers… which is of you kept back by fraud, crieth: and the cries… are entered into the ears of the Lord of sabaoth” — the witnesses mark the cry of the defrauded laborer.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the law. Robertson: “the hire” (misthos) of the workers who mowed; “Lord of sabaoth” (of hosts). Adam Clarke: the law required wages paid the same day (Leviticus 19:13; Deuteronomy 24:15). The Reformed Calvin: cruelty, the companion of avarice. Wesley: those sins cry to God of which human laws are silent.

The verse names the rich man’s fraud. The withheld wages of the laborers cry out, and their cries reach the Lord of hosts. Defrauded poor have a Judge.

So the witnesses see James charge the rich with a further crime: defrauding the laborer of his wages. The witnesses note the specific sin: “the hire of the labourers… which is of you kept back by fraud” — the rich had withheld or fraudulently delayed the wages of the poor who reaped their fields, breaking the merciful law that required a hired man’s wages be paid the same day, lest he and his family go hungry. They mark the personification: the withheld wages “crieth” — the very money cries out to heaven for justice, an injustice that will not be silent. They observe the second cry: “the cries of them which have reaped are entered into the ears of the Lord of sabaoth” — the laborers themselves cry to God, and their cries reach the “Lord of hosts,” the title of God’s irresistible power and command of armies, who hears the oppressed and will avenge them. They note Wesley’s point that such sins, on which human laws are often silent, cry the louder to God. They observe Calvin’s sense that this is the cruelty which avarice breeds, peculiarly odious because it robs the weak of their bread. They note the comfort to the poor: their wrong is heard in the highest court. The verse thus exposes the rich man’s oppression: the unpaid wages and the laborers’ cries have reached the Lord of hosts — a Judge who will requite the fraud the next verses press toward judgment.
5:
On James 5:5 — “Ye have lived in pleasure on the earth, and been wanton; ye have nourished your hearts, as in a day of slaughter” — the witnesses mark the luxury and self-indulgence of the rich.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the words. Robertson: “ye have lived delicately” (etryphesate, soft living), “taken your pleasure” (espatalesate, wanton). Ellicott: “ye nourished your hearts in a day of slaughter.” Adam Clarke: lived luxuriously and lasciviously. The Reformed Calvin: luxury and sinful gratifications, abusing abundance.

The verse charges them with wanton luxury. They lived in pleasure and self-indulgence, fattening themselves for slaughter. Their feasting prepares them for judgment.

So the witnesses see James add to fraud the charge of wanton, self-indulgent luxury. The witnesses note the first description: “ye have lived in pleasure on the earth” — a soft, delicate, voluptuous life, given over to ease and indulgence; “and been wanton” — lived lasciviously, without restraint, gratifying every appetite. They mark that this luxury was fed by the very fraud just named: they feasted while their laborers went unpaid. They observe the striking final image: “ye have nourished your hearts, as in a day of slaughter” — they pampered and fattened themselves, like cattle gorged before the day they are killed; the very richness of their living, all unknowing, was fitting them for the slaughter of judgment. They note Ellicott’s and the expositors’ sense of the grim irony: the day of feasting is the day of slaughter; their indulgence is the fattening for the kill. They observe that this is the portrait of the godless rich at the end — eating, drinking, and making merry, heedless that judgment is at the door. They note the contrast with the starving poor they had defrauded. The verse thus completes the indictment of their living: a wanton, luxurious self-indulgence that has only fattened them, like beasts, for the day of slaughter — the judgment whose final count, the killing of the just, the next verse names.

So the witnesses see James add to fraud the charge of wanton luxury. The first description: “ye have lived in pleasure on the earth” — a soft, delicate, voluptuous life; “and been wanton” — lived lasciviously, without restraint. This luxury was fed by the very fraud just named: they feasted while their labourers went unpaid. The striking final image: “ye have nourished your hearts, as in a day of slaughter” — they pampered and fattened themselves, like cattle gorged before the day they are killed; the very richness of their living, all unknowing, was fitting them for the slaughter of judgment. The grim irony: the day of feasting is the day of slaughter; their indulgence is the fattening for the kill, in contrast to the starving poor they had defrauded.
6:
On James 5:6 — “Ye have condemned and killed the just; and he doth not resist you” — the witnesses mark the climax of the rich man’s guilt, and a disputed reference.

The grammatical-historical commentators divide. Robertson: “ye have killed the righteous one” (ton dikaion), the rich controlling the courts; the poor cannot resist. The Reformed Calvin: the rich by power oppressed and destroyed the poor and weak. Wesley and some: “that Just One,” Christ (Acts 3:14), and James the Just. Ellicott notes the harshness if applied to Christ.

The verse names their gravest sin. They condemned and killed the just, who did not resist. Oppression reaches even to judicial murder.

So the witnesses see James reach the height of the rich man’s guilt: the condemning and killing of the just. The witnesses note the charge: “ye have condemned and killed the just” — the oppressive rich, controlling the courts of justice, used their power to procure the legal condemnation and even the death of the righteous poor; whether by direct violence or by perverting justice, they destroyed the innocent. They mark the pathos of the close: “and he doth not resist you” — the just one, poor and powerless, offers no resistance, having no means to withstand; his very helplessness aggravates their cruelty and leaves his cause to God. They observe the debated reference of “the just” (singular). Most, with Calvin and Robertson, take it generically or collectively: the righteous poor, the class of innocent victims the rich oppressed and killed. Others, with Wesley, hear an allusion to “that Just One,” Christ himself, whom the rulers condemned and slew, and beyond him the righteous like James the Just. They note that on either reading the charge stands: the powerful had shed innocent blood. They observe that the unresisting meekness of the victim throws the guilt wholly on the oppressor. The verse thus crowns the indictment of the rich: they have gone so far as to condemn and kill the unresisting just — a wrong that cries to the God who, as the next verses promise the oppressed, is coming.
7:
On James 5:7 — “Be patient therefore, brethren, unto the coming of the Lord. Behold, the husbandman waiteth for the precious fruit… until he receive the early and latter rain” — the witnesses mark the call to patience like the farmer’s.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the turn. Robertson: “be patient” (makrothymesate), a corollary from the judgment on the rich; the farmer’s long-suffering. Adam Clarke: the coming of the Lord to judge the Jewish nation, soon to follow. The Puritan Henry: the husbandman waits for a crop; will not you wait for a crown? Wesley notes the former and latter rain.

The verse turns to comfort the oppressed. Be patient till the Lord comes, as the farmer waits for his crop and the rains. Patience awaits a certain harvest.

So the witnesses see James turn from denouncing the rich to comforting their victims with a call to patience. The witnesses note the “therefore”: because the Lord is coming to judge the oppressors (vv. 1–6), the wronged may patiently endure; the assurance of judgment is the ground of patience. They mark the address “brethren” — the suffering poor, whom James owns as family, in contrast to the rich. They observe the command: “be patient… unto the coming of the Lord” — bear the wrongs without murmuring or revenge, looking to the Lord’s advent (his coming to judge, including the imminent judgment on Jerusalem, and his final coming). They note the homely illustration: “the husbandman waiteth for the precious fruit of the earth… until he receive the early and latter rain” — the farmer, having sown, waits with long patience through the seasons, looking for the autumn and spring rains that ripen the harvest; he cannot hurry the crop but waits in hope. They observe Henry’s telling application: if the farmer waits patiently for a crop of corn, will not the believer wait for a crown of glory? They note that the precious fruit is worth the waiting. The verse thus sets the pattern of patience: as the farmer waits through the rains for his harvest, so the oppressed are to wait for the coming of the Lord — establishing their hearts, the next verse adds, for his coming is near.
8:
On James 5:8 — “Be ye also patient; stablish your hearts: for the coming of the Lord draweth nigh” — the witnesses mark the call to steadfast hearts.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the verbs. Robertson: “stablish” (sterixate, make stable, 1 Thessalonians 3:13); “is at hand” (eggiken). Adam Clarke: take courage, do not sink under trials; he is already on his way. The Reformed Calvin: correct the softness of heart that weakens us. Wesley: his coming to destroy Jerusalem is nigh.

The verse applies the farmer’s patience. Be patient and establish your hearts, for the Lord’s coming is near. Nearness of the advent steadies the soul.

So the witnesses see James apply the farmer’s example directly: be patient, and let it strengthen the heart. The witnesses note the “also”: “be ye also patient” — as the husbandman waits, so must the suffering believers wait, with the same long-suffering through their season of trial. They mark the added charge: “stablish your hearts” — make firm, steady, and resolute the inner man; let the purpose and faith be unwavering, not sinking under the weight of oppression nor growing fretful. They observe Calvin’s point that this corrects the softness of heart that would otherwise weaken them and break their perseverance; the heart must be braced. They note Clarke’s encouragement to take courage and not sink under trial. They observe the great motive: “for the coming of the Lord draweth nigh” — the Lord’s advent is near at hand; whether his coming in judgment on the persecuting nation (soon to fall) or his final coming, the nearness of it is the ground of steadfastness. They note that the expectation of his speedy coming both comforts the oppressed and steadies the wavering: deliverance and reward are not far off. The verse thus turns the farmer’s patience into a charge to the heart: be patient and stand firm, for the Lord is near — a nearness that, the next verse warns, makes murmuring against one another the more unfitting.

So the witnesses see James apply the farmer’s example directly. The “also”: “be ye also patient” — as the husbandman waits, so must the suffering believers wait through their season of trial. The added charge: “stablish your hearts” — make firm, steady, and resolute the inner man, not sinking under oppression nor growing fretful. Calvin notes this corrects the softness of heart that would weaken them and break their perseverance. The great motive: “for the coming of the Lord draweth nigh” — whether his coming in judgment on the persecuting nation soon to fall, or his final coming, the nearness of it is the ground of steadfastness. The expectation of his speedy coming both comforts the oppressed and steadies the wavering: deliverance and reward are not far off.
9:
On James 5:9 — “Grudge not one against another, brethren, lest ye be condemned: behold, the judge standeth before the door” — the witnesses mark the warning against murmuring.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the term. Robertson: “murmur not” (me stenazete, stop groaning against one another). Ellicott: “grudge” has changed meaning; rather “murmur not.” Adam Clarke: groan not, grumble not through impatience; leave it in God’s hands. Wesley: “the judge standeth before the door, hearing every word.”

The verse warns against mutual murmuring. Do not grumble against one another, for the Judge is at the door. Impatience under trial turns into fault-finding.

So the witnesses see James guard the suffering against a peculiar danger of trial: turning their impatience on one another. The witnesses note the command: “grudge not one against another” — better, “murmur” or “groan not”; under shared affliction believers are apt to grow fretful and to vent their distress in complaints, sighing and grumbling against their brethren. They mark that the word means an inward groan of ill-humor breaking out against others; the pressure of oppression must not be allowed to sour them toward one another. They observe the warning: “lest ye be condemned” — such censorious murmuring incurs judgment; the impatience that finds fault with brethren is itself a fault that God will judge. They note the solemn motive: “behold, the judge standeth before the door” — the Lord, the Judge, is at hand, as it were standing at the very door, hearing every word and marking every thought; his nearness, just urged as comfort, is now a check on murmuring. They observe Clarke’s counsel to leave all wrongs in God’s hands rather than vent them in imprecations. They note the gentleness of “brethren,” reminding them of their bond. The verse thus adds a needful caution to the call for patience: bear trial without grumbling against one another, for the Judge stands at the door — and look, the next verse says, to the prophets as patterns of endurance.

So the witnesses see James guard the suffering against turning their impatience on one another. The command: “grudge not one against another” — better, “murmur” or “groan not”; under shared affliction believers are apt to grow fretful and vent their distress in complaints against their brethren, an inward groan of ill-humour breaking out. The warning: “lest ye be condemned” — such censorious murmuring incurs judgment; the impatience that finds fault with brethren is itself a fault God will judge. The solemn motive: “behold, the judge standeth before the door” — the Lord at hand, hearing every word and marking every thought; his nearness, just urged as comfort, is now a check on murmuring. Clarke counsels leaving all wrongs in God’s hands.
10:
On James 5:10 — “Take, my brethren, the prophets, who have spoken in the name of the Lord, for an example of suffering affliction, and of patience” — the witnesses mark the prophets as patterns of patient endurance.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the example. Robertson: “for an example” (hypodeigma), a copy to be imitated (John 13:15). Barnes: the prophets, a much-persecuted race, to encourage patience. Ellicott: the bright ones in the cloud of witnesses (Hebrews 12:1). Wesley: the very men who gloried in having prophets could not bear their message.

The verse points to the prophets’ example. They suffered affliction yet endured with patience. The honored servants of God bore wrongs patiently.

So the witnesses see James hold up the prophets as a pattern of suffering borne with patience. The witnesses note the appeal to example: “take… the prophets… for an example” — a model to be copied; in their own trials the readers are pointed to those who trod the same path before them. They mark the dignity of these examples: “who have spoken in the name of the Lord” — men highly honored, God’s own spokesmen, to whom he revealed his secrets; yet even they were not exempt from affliction. They observe the double lesson: “suffering affliction, and of patience” — the prophets both endured grievous persecution and bore it with patience; they are examples at once of what the readers suffer and of how to suffer it. They note Wesley’s pointed irony that the very people who gloried in having prophets could not bear their message but persecuted them; neither their holiness nor their high commission shielded them from suffering. They observe Ellicott’s placing of them among the bright cloud of witnesses. They note the comfort: the sufferer is in noble company, classed with God’s honored servants. The verse thus offers the prophets as encouragement: God’s most honored messengers suffered affliction and endured it with patience — a patience that, the next verse shows in Job, ends in blessing.

So the witnesses see James hold up the prophets as a pattern of suffering borne with patience. The appeal to example: “take the prophets for an example” — a model to be copied; in their own trials the readers are pointed to those who trod the same path before them. Their dignity: “who have spoken in the name of the Lord” — men highly honoured, God’s own spokesmen, yet not exempt from affliction. The double lesson: “suffering affliction, and of patience” — examples at once of what the readers suffer and of how to suffer it. Wesley notes the irony that the very people who gloried in having prophets persecuted them; neither their holiness nor their high commission shielded them. The sufferer is in noble company, classed with God’s honoured servants.
11:
On James 5:11 — “Behold, we count them happy which endure. Ye have heard of the patience of Job, and have seen the end of the Lord; that the Lord is very pitiful, and of tender mercy” — the witnesses mark Job’s endurance and the Lord’s merciful end.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the example. Robertson: “we call blessed” (makarizomen) those who endure (Daniel 12:12). Adam Clarke: blessed are ye when men persecute you (Matthew 5:11). The Reformed Calvin: no one was overwhelmed with troubles so hard as Job, yet he emerged. Wesley: “the end which the Lord gave him.”

The verse crowns the call with Job’s example. Those who endure are blessed; Job’s patience saw the Lord’s merciful end. Endurance is vindicated by God’s tender outcome.

So the witnesses see James crown the call to patience with the beatitude of endurance and the supreme example of Job. The witnesses note the verdict: “we count them happy which endure” — we pronounce blessed those who hold out under suffering, echoing Christ’s beatitude on the persecuted; present trial is the path to true blessedness. They mark the chosen example: “ye have heard of the patience of Job” — no one, as Calvin observes, was overwhelmed with troubles so hard and various as Job, who lost all yet did not let go his integrity; he is the great pattern of endurance. They observe the outcome: “ye have seen the end of the Lord” — the end, the issue, which the Lord gave Job; after his patient suffering God restored him and blessed his latter end above the beginning. They note the lesson drawn: “that the Lord is very pitiful, and of tender mercy” — the merciful outcome of Job’s trial reveals God’s character: full of compassion and tender mercy, who does not afflict willingly but brings his servants through to blessing. They observe that the end crowns the endurance: the sufferer who holds out will find God merciful in the issue. The verse thus seals the call to patience: those who endure are blessed, as Job’s patience and the Lord’s tender end prove — before James turns to particular counsels, beginning with the tongue and the oath.
12:
On James 5:12 — “But above all things, my brethren, swear not, neither by heaven, neither by the earth… but let your yea be yea; and your nay, nay; lest ye fall into condemnation” — the witnesses mark the warning against swearing.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the source. Robertson: “above all things” (pro panton), an allusion to Jesus’ words (Matthew 5:34–37). The Puritan Henry: the sin of swearing is cautioned against. Wesley: the Jews were notoriously guilty of common swearing, even by creatures; the apostle forbids these oaths and all swearing in common conversation. Ellicott notes the perplexity over the lawfulness of oaths.

The verse forbids profane swearing. Above all, do not swear by heaven or earth; let yea be yea. Plain truthfulness replaces the oath.

So the witnesses see James, echoing his Master, set a strong caution against swearing. The witnesses note the emphasis: “above all things… swear not” — a matter of special weight, drawn almost verbatim from Christ’s teaching in the Sermon on the Mount. They mark the prohibition: “neither by heaven, neither by the earth, neither by any other oath” — the rash, profane swearing by creatures common among the Jews, who (as Wesley notes) swore freely by heaven, earth, and the like in ordinary speech, fancying such oaths less binding. They observe the positive rule: “let your yea be yea; and your nay, nay” — let plain, simple truthfulness govern speech; a Christian’s bare word should be as good as another man’s oath, needing no swearing to support it. They note the question the expositors raise — whether this forbids all oaths whatever (as the Essenes and some sects held) or chiefly the rash, profane, conversational swearing; most take it to condemn light and irreverent oaths in common talk, not the solemn judicial oath. They observe the warning: “lest ye fall into condemnation” — careless swearing profanes God’s name and brings guilt. They note that impatience under trial often vents itself in rash oaths, which links this to the foregoing. The verse thus cautions against profane swearing: let the Christian’s plain word suffice, lest careless oaths bring condemnation — and James turns next to the better outlets of feeling, prayer and praise.
13:
On James 5:13 — “Is any among you afflicted? let him pray. Is any merry? let him sing psalms” — the witnesses mark prayer and praise as the answer to every condition.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the pairing. Robertson: “is any suffering?” (kakopathei), “let him pray” (proseuchestho). Ellicott: the whole life revolving around the throne of God, in night or day. The Reformed Calvin: there is no time in which God does not invite us to himself; affliction stimulates prayer, prosperity supplies praise.

The verse directs every mood to God. The afflicted should pray, the cheerful sing psalms. Sorrow and joy alike turn to God.

So the witnesses see James direct each condition of life to its fitting expression toward God. The witnesses note the first: “is any among you afflicted? let him pray” — the one suffering trouble, sickness, or distress should turn to prayer; affliction is to drive the soul to God for help, not to murmuring or rash oaths. They mark the second: “is any merry? let him sing psalms” — the one who is cheerful and glad should express it in praise, singing psalms to God; joy is to be sanctified by being turned heavenward. They observe Calvin’s beautiful point that there is no time in which God does not invite us to himself: affliction stimulates us to prayer, prosperity supplies us with occasion to praise; both moods have their godward outlet. They note Ellicott’s image of the whole life revolving around the throne of God, whether in the night of sorrow or the day of gladness. They observe the contrast with the world, which in sorrow despairs and in mirth forgets God; the Christian in both remembers him. They note that this corrects the misuse of feeling just warned against: instead of impatient groaning or profane oaths, let sorrow become prayer and joy become song. The verse thus sets the rhythm of the godly life: prayer in affliction, praise in gladness — before James gives a fuller direction for the special case of sickness.

So the witnesses see James direct each condition of life to its fitting expression toward God. The first: “is any among you afflicted? let him pray” — the one suffering trouble or distress should turn to prayer; affliction is to drive the soul to God, not to murmuring or rash oaths. The second: “is any merry? let him sing psalms” — the cheerful should express it in praise; joy is sanctified by being turned heavenward. Calvin’s beautiful point: there is no time in which God does not invite us to himself; affliction stimulates prayer, prosperity supplies praise. Unlike the world, which in sorrow despairs and in mirth forgets God, the Christian in both remembers him — sorrow becoming prayer, joy becoming song.
14:
On James 5:14 — “Is any sick among you? let him call for the elders of the church; and let them pray over him, anointing him with oil in the name of the Lord” — the witnesses mark the care of the sick, and a disputed rite.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the elders and the oil. Robertson: “is any sick?” (asthenei); “let him call for” (proskalesastho) the elders. Ellicott: “the elders” — the presbyters; bishop and presbyter identical in the apostolic age. The Reformed Calvin: the gift of healing as yet continued; the oil a symbol. Wesley: this gift remained after the others were withdrawn, and was designed to remain.

The verse directs the sick to the elders. Let them pray over him and anoint him with oil in the Lord’s name. The church’s prayer attends the sick.

So the witnesses see James give a fuller direction for the special trial of sickness. The witnesses note the action enjoined: “is any sick among you? let him call for the elders of the church” — the sick believer is to send for the church’s elders (the presbyters), the recognized officers, that the prayer of the church through its leaders may be brought to his aid. They mark the twofold ministry: “let them pray over him” — prayer is the heart of the act; “anointing him with oil in the name of the Lord” — accompanied by anointing with oil, done in the Lord’s name and authority. They observe the much-debated nature of this oil. Some, with Calvin and Wesley, connect it with the gift of miraculous healing then in the church (as in Mark 6:13), the oil a visible symbol attending that gift; Wesley holds the gift was designed to remain. Others have seen in the oil a medicinal use, oil being a common remedy. They note that the Reformed expositors firmly reject the later sacramental misuse of this verse as “extreme unction,” a rite for the dying — for here the oil is for healing the living, not preparing the dying, as Ellicott and others insist against the medieval reading. They observe that the constant element is prayer; the oil is its accompaniment, not its substitute. The verse thus directs the sick to the church’s prayer and anointing — whose efficacy, the next verse declares, lies in the prayer of faith.
15:
On James 5:15 — “And the prayer of faith shall save the sick, and the Lord shall raise him up; and if he have committed sins, they shall be forgiven him” — the witnesses mark the power of believing prayer.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the promise. Robertson: “the prayer of faith” (he euche tes pisteos); “shall save” (sosei), make well — “no reference here to salvation of the soul.” Adam Clarke: God often makes these means of recovery, yet sometimes faith and prayer are ineffectual when restoration would harm the soul. The Reformed Calvin connects the promise with faith. Wesley: saved from his sickness, and sin forgiven.

The verse states the efficacy. The prayer of faith shall save the sick, the Lord raise him, and sins be forgiven. Believing prayer brings healing and pardon.

So the witnesses see James locate the healing power not in the oil but in the believing prayer. The witnesses note the agent: “the prayer of faith” — prayer marked by faith, offered by the elders and joined by the sick man’s own trust; this, not the oil, is what avails. They mark the promise: “shall save the sick, and the Lord shall raise him up” — here, as Robertson insists, “save” means make well, restore to health; the Lord himself is the healer who raises the sick man from his bed. They observe the careful qualification the expositors add: this is no unconditional guarantee that every sick believer will recover, for, as Clarke notes, faith and prayer are sometimes not granted recovery when God sees that restoration would harm the soul; the prayer of faith submits to God’s will. They note the spiritual blessing joined: “if he have committed sins, they shall be forgiven him” — where sin may have occasioned the sickness, or in any case, pardon accompanies the healing; the whole man, body and soul, is cared for. They observe that prayer thus reaches to both recovery and forgiveness. They note that the verse keeps the focus on faith and on the Lord as the true physician. The verse thus declares the efficacy of believing prayer: it saves the sick and brings forgiveness — a promise that leads James to urge mutual confession and prayer among all the brethren.
16:
On James 5:16 — “Confess your faults one to another, and pray one for another, that ye may be healed. The effectual fervent prayer of a righteous man availeth much” — the witnesses mark mutual confession and the power of righteous prayer.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the practice. Robertson: “confess” (exomologeisthe); public confession of certain sins greatly helpful, confession to God assumed. Ellicott: the Catholic auricular meaning cannot be established from the text. Adam Clarke: social confession humbles the soul. Wesley: “not to the elders… to any who can pray in faith… it is nowhere commanded” to confess to a priest.

The verse enjoins mutual confession and prayer. Confess faults and pray for one another; the righteous man’s prayer avails much. Open hearts and fervent prayer bring healing.

So the witnesses see James broaden the ministry of prayer from the elders to the whole brotherhood. The witnesses note the mutual duty: “confess your faults one to another” — chiefly the sins committed against one another, openly acknowledged for reconciliation, and (in the context of sickness) the unburdening of conscience that the sick may be helped. They mark the Protestant expositors’ firm point, against the Roman use of this verse for auricular confession to a priest: the text enjoins mutual confession among brethren, not confession to a priest; Wesley notes it is to any who can pray in faith, not commanded to the elders, and Ellicott that the sacramental meaning cannot be established from the Greek. They observe the joined duty: “and pray one for another, that ye may be healed” — confession opens the way for intercession, and mutual prayer brings healing of body and soul. They note the great encouragement: “the effectual fervent prayer of a righteous man availeth much” — the earnest, energetic prayer of a righteous person has great power with God, accomplishing much. They observe that this lifts prayer from a form to a force: when offered fervently by the righteous, it is mighty in effect. The verse thus widens and energizes the ministry of prayer: let the brethren confess and intercede for one another, for the fervent prayer of the righteous avails much — a power the next verses prove from Elijah.
17:
On James 5:17 — “Elias was a man subject to like passions as we are, and he prayed earnestly that it might not rain: and it rained not on the earth by the space of three years and six months” — the witnesses mark Elijah as proof of prayer’s power.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the example. Robertson: “of like passions with us” (homoiopathes, suffering the like, Acts 14:15); “he prayed fervently” (proseuche proseuxato, a Hebrew idiom). Adam Clarke: a human being just as ourselves. Ellicott: James supplies a detail not in 1 Kings 17:1. Wesley: “naturally as weak and sinful as we are.”

The verse proves prayer’s power by Elijah. A man like us, he prayed and the rain ceased three years and a half. Earnest prayer moved heaven.

So the witnesses see James prove the might of righteous prayer by the example of Elijah. The witnesses note the encouraging emphasis on Elijah’s humanity: “a man subject to like passions as we are” — not a superhuman saint exempt from frailty, but a man of the same nature, the same weaknesses and feelings as ourselves; this is stressed so that none think such power of prayer beyond ordinary believers. They mark the prayer: “he prayed earnestly that it might not rain” — the Hebrew-flavored idiom (“with prayer he prayed”) marks the intensity and earnestness of his praying. They observe the effect: “it rained not on the earth by the space of three years and six months” — heaven itself was shut at the prophet’s request, the drought lasting three and a half years; James supplies the detail of the prayer behind the narrative of Kings. They note Calvin’s sense of the greatness of it: that God should make heaven, as it were, subject to the prayers of Elijah, obeying his word. They observe that the whole point is that a man just like us, by earnest prayer, wielded such power; therefore the fervent prayer of the righteous (v. 16) is no idle word. They note the encouragement to ordinary believers. The verse thus grounds the promise of prayer’s power in Elijah: a man of like passions, by earnest prayer, shut heaven — and, as the next verse adds, opened it again.
18:
On James 5:18 — “And he prayed again, and the heaven gave rain, and the earth brought forth her fruit” — the witnesses mark the second prayer that restored the rain.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the sequel. Robertson: “gave rain” (hyeton edoken), the LXX idiom of God or heaven (1 Kings 18:1); “brought forth” (eblastesen). Adam Clarke: the second prayer, when Elijah cast himself down with his face between his knees (1 Kings 18:42). Wesley: “he prayed again, when idolatry was abolished.”

The verse completes the example. Elijah prayed again, and heaven gave rain and the earth her fruit. The same prayer that shut heaven opened it.

So the witnesses see James complete the example of Elijah with the prayer that brought the rain back. The witnesses note the balance: “he prayed again” — the same prophet who by prayer had shut heaven now by prayer opens it; the expositors find this second praying in the narrative where Elijah cast himself down on Carmel, his face between his knees, in the very posture of earnest intercession. They mark the result: “the heaven gave rain, and the earth brought forth her fruit” — at his prayer the long drought broke, the rain returned, and the barren land, revived, yielded its harvest again; prayer both withheld and restored the blessing. They observe that the two prayers together — one shutting, one opening heaven — make the full demonstration: prayer is mighty in both directions, to withhold judgment’s and to bring mercy’s blessing. They note Wesley’s framing that he prayed for drought when idolatry covered the land, and for rain when idolatry was abolished — his praying aligned with God’s purposes. They observe that the whole example crowns verse 16: if a man like us could do this, the fervent prayer of the righteous truly avails much. The verse thus seals the lesson of Elijah: his second prayer gave rain and fruit — before James turns, in closing, to the saving of a wandering soul.

So the witnesses see James complete the example of Elijah with the prayer that brought the rain back. The balance: “he prayed again” — the same prophet who by prayer shut heaven now by prayer opens it; the expositors find this second praying where Elijah cast himself down on Carmel, his face between his knees. The result: “the heaven gave rain, and the earth brought forth her fruit” — the long drought broke, the rain returned, and the barren land yielded its harvest again. The two prayers together — one shutting, one opening heaven — make the full demonstration: prayer is mighty in both directions. Wesley frames it that Elijah prayed for drought when idolatry covered the land, and for rain when it was abolished — his praying aligned with God’s purposes.
19:
On James 5:19 — “Brethren, if any of you do err from the truth, and one convert him” — the witnesses mark the closing concern for the wanderer.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the case. Robertson: “if any one among you do err” (planethe, go astray, Matthew 18:12); “from the truth” (apo tes aletheias). Ellicott: the last and dearest of the apostle’s remarks, on conversion. The Puritan Henry: there is some doctrinal mistake at the bottom of every practical error.

The verse opens the final exhortation. If one wanders from the truth and another turns him back. The recovery of the straying is the closing concern.

So the witnesses see James close his epistle with a tender concern for the wandering brother and the one who restores him. The witnesses note the case supposed: “if any of you do err from the truth” — if any believer strays, whether by embracing doctrinal error or falling into sinful practice; both are a wandering from the truth, which is at once a creed to be believed and a way to be walked. They mark Henry’s shrewd observation that there is usually some doctrinal mistake at the bottom of every practical error; wandering in life and in belief are linked. They observe the gracious act: “and one convert him” — if another, in love, turns the wanderer back, recovering him from his error to the truth; the work of restoration belongs to the brethren, not only to officers. They note that Ellicott calls this the last and dearest of the apostle’s remarks, fittingly closing his plain-speaking, loving letter with the recovery of the straying. They observe that the whole epistle, so full of practical correction, ends not in condemnation but in the hope of restoration. They note the responsibility laid on every believer to seek the wanderer. The verse thus opens the closing word: when one strays from the truth and another turns him back — an act whose great worth the final verse declares.

So the witnesses see James close with a tender concern for the wandering brother. The case supposed: “if any of you do err from the truth” — if any believer strays, whether by doctrinal error or sinful practice; both are a wandering from the truth, which is at once a creed to be believed and a way to be walked. Henry’s shrewd observation: there is usually some doctrinal mistake at the bottom of every practical error. The gracious act: “and one convert him” — if another, in love, turns the wanderer back; the work of restoration belongs to the brethren, not only to officers. Ellicott calls this the last and dearest of the apostle’s remarks, fittingly ending a letter so full of correction not in condemnation but in the hope of restoration.
20:
On James 5:20 — “Let him know, that he which converteth the sinner from the error of his way shall save a soul from death, and shall hide a multitude of sins” — the witnesses mark the blessedness of saving a soul.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the reward. Robertson: “he which converteth” (ho epistrepsas); the conclusion of verse 19. Adam Clarke: he saves a soul from eternal death, and hides a multitude of sins (Proverbs 10:12; 1 Peter 4:8). Ellicott: “know ye” — be absolutely sure of this. Wesley: “how much more value than the body!”

The verse crowns the epistle with a promise. He who converts a sinner saves a soul from death and hides a multitude of sins. To restore the straying is a glorious work.

So the witnesses see James close his letter by setting the highest value on the recovery of a soul. The witnesses note the call to certainty: “let him know” (or “know ye”) — let the one who restores a wanderer be absolutely sure of this great truth, for his encouragement to the work. They mark the first part of the reward: “he which converteth the sinner from the error of his way shall save a soul from death” — the soul, of how much more value than the body, is rescued from death, the eternal death that the sinner’s course was leading to; to turn a straying soul back is to save it from perishing. They observe the second part: “and shall hide a multitude of sins” — the many sins of the converted are covered, blotted out, no more remembered to his condemnation, for in his conversion they are forgiven; the proverb “love covereth a multitude of sins” is fulfilled in this saving love. They note the expositors’ sense that this crowns the whole epistle: of all good works, the recovery of a soul is among the greatest, saving from death and securing pardon. They observe that the letter, so full of stern reproof, ends on this gracious, hopeful note. The verse thus seals James’s message: to turn a sinner from his error is to save a soul from death and hide a multitude of sins — the blessed close of a thoroughly practical epistle.
